mnemonics: COPY CAT: Copy Constructor Avoids The Problem (of shallow copies)
OVERLOAD: Offering Various Entry Routes, Leaving All Defaults Behind
keyPoints: A constructor can be overloaded to provide multiple ways to initialize an object, including a default constructor with no parameters.
A copy constructor is a special overloaded constructor that creates a new object as a copy of an existing one, taking a constant reference to an object of the same class as its parameter.
If you don't define a copy constructor, C++ provides a default one that performs a shallow (member-wise) copy, which can cause problems if your class manages resources like dynamic memory.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to define a copy constructor when the class contains raw pointers, leading to double-free errors and shallow copies.
Not making the parameter of the copy constructor a const reference, which can cause compilation errors and inefficient copying.
Defining a copy constructor that only performs a shallow copy when a deep copy is actually required for correct behavior.
